title: Your first generic function
instruction:
A generic function uses `<T>` as a type parameter that gets filled in at the call site. Write a generic `identity` function and use it with different types.

Run this.
successMessage: `T` is inferred from the argument — `identity(42)` infers `T = number`. No casting or `any` needed.

title: Generic arrays
instruction:
Write a generic function that returns the first element of any array. `T[]` means 'array of T'.

Run this.
successMessage: The return type `T | undefined` handles the empty array case. TypeScript infers `T` from what you pass in.

title: Generic constraints with extends
instruction:
Use `T extends SomeType` to restrict which types are allowed. This lets you safely access properties that exist on the constraint.

Run this.
successMessage: `T extends { length: number }` guarantees `.length` exists. Works for strings, arrays, or any object with a length property.

title: Multiple type parameters
instruction:
Functions can have multiple independent type parameters. Each is inferred separately.

Run this.
successMessage: `A` and `B` are inferred independently — `zip([1,2,3], ["a","b","c"])` gives `[number, string][]`.
